attributes.stem:
A 27-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a two-hour history of sudden onset shortness of breath and pleuritic chest pain. She contracted COVID-19 three weeks ago, experiencing moderate symptoms including fever, cough, runny nose, and loss of smell. Her recovery had been progressing well until the onset of these new symptoms. She has also noticed some blood in her sputum. She has received three doses of the COVID-19 vaccine. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications except for the combined oral contraceptive pill. She has no known allergies.

On examination, she appears distressed. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 105/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 26/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 37.4°C. On auscultation, her lungs are clear bilaterally and heart sounds are dual.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms suggestive of a pulmonary embolism (PE), which is the most likely diagnosis given her recent COVID-19 infection and use of the combined oral contraceptive pill. The risk of PE is increased for up to 6 months following COVID-19 infection.

CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) is the most appropriate investigation in this case. It is the gold standard for diagnosing PE, especially in patients with a high pre-test probability. CTPA allows direct visualization of emboli in the pulmonary arteries, providing crucial information for diagnosis and treatment planning.

Nasopharyngeal viral PCR is not appropriate as the primary investigation here. While it could detect ongoing COVID-19 infection, it would not explain the sudden onset of symptoms or rule out a potentially life-threatening PE.

Chest X-ray is a useful initial investigation for dyspnoea, but it is not diagnostic for PE. It may show non-specific changes or be normal in PE cases. While it might be performed as part of the initial workup, it is not the most appropriate investigation given the high suspicion of PE.

D-dimer testing is most useful in patients with a low pre-test probability of PE to rule out the condition. In this case, the patient has at least a moderate risk based on her Wells' score (tachycardia, hemoptysis, recent COVID-19 infection, and oral contraceptive use as risk factors). D-dimer is inappropriate as it's best aimed at helping rule out PE in low risk individuals. Moreover, D-dimer levels can be elevated due to recent COVID-19 infection, reducing its specificity in this scenario.

Ventilation-perfusion scan is an alternative to CTPA for diagnosing PE, particularly in patients with contraindications to CTPA (e.g., contrast allergy or renal impairment). However, it is less accurate than CTPA, especially in patients with underlying lung disease. Given this patient's recent COVID-19 infection, which may have affected her lung parenchyma, CTPA would be preferred for its superior diagnostic accuracy.

In summary, while all these investigations may have a role in the patient's overall assessment, CTPA is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test given the high suspicion of PE and the need for rapid, accurate diagnosis to guide immediate management.
learningPoints: Following COVID-19 infection, patients are at an increased risk for pulmonary embolism for up to 6 months.
CT pulmonary angiogram is the gold standard investigation for diagnosing pulmonary embolism in patients with a high pre-test probability.
The combined oral contraceptive pill is a risk factor for pulmonary embolism.
references: Katsoularis, I., Fonseca-Rodríguez, O., Farrington, P., Jerndal, H., Lundevaller, E., Sund, M., Lindmark, K. and Fors Connolly, A., 2022. Risks of deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and bleeding after covid-19: nationwide self-controlled cases series and matched cohort study. BMJ, (337), p.e069590.

Modi S, Deisler R, Gozel K, Reicks P, Irwin E, Brunsvold M, Banton K, Beilman GJ. Wells criteria for DVT is a reliable clinical tool to assess the risk of deep venous thrombosis in trauma patients. World J Emerg Surg. 2016 Jun 8;11:24. doi: 10.1186/s13017-016-0078-1. PMID: 27279896; PMCID: PMC4898382.

Bhatia KD, Ambati C, Dhaliwal R, Paschkewitz R, Hsu E, Ho B, Young A, Emmett L. SPECT-CT/VQ versus CTPA for diagnosing pulmonary embolus and other lung pathology: Pre-existing lung disease should not be a contraindication. J Med Imaging Radiat Oncol. 2016 Aug;60(4):492-7. doi: 10.1111/1754-9485.12471. Epub 2016 May 11. PMID: 27461384.
